abstract: Large language models are increasingly post-trained with reinforcement learning in verifiable domains such as code and math. Yet, current methods for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) learn only from a scalar outcome reward per attempt, creating a severe credit-assignment bottleneck. Many verifiable environments actually provide rich textual feedback, such as runtime errors or judge evaluations, that explain why an attempt failed. We formalize this setting as reinforcement learning with rich feedback and introduce Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO), which converts tokenized feedback into a dense learning signal without any external teacher or explicit reward model. SDPO treats the current model conditioned on feedback as a self-teacher and distills its feedback-informed next-token predictions back into the policy. In this way, SDPO leverages the model's ability to retrospectively identify its own mistakes in-context. Across scientific reasoning, tool use, and competitive programming on LiveCodeBench v6, SDPO improves sample efficiency and final accuracy over strong RLVR baselines. Notably, SDPO also outperforms baselines in standard RLVR environments that only return scalar feedback by using successful rollouts as implicit feedback for failed attempts. Finally, applying SDPO to individual questions at test time accelerates discovery on difficult binary-reward tasks, achieving the same discovery probability as best-of-k sampling or multi-turn conversations with 3x fewer attempts.

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs) possess an inherent ability for in-context learning, allowing them to effectively self-correct and provide dense learning signals when re-prompted with their own failures and rich feedback.

Dense, token-level credit assignment is critical for improving both learning efficiency and the quality of reasoning in LLMs, leading to more concise and accurate responses than sequence-level rewards.

SDPO, as an on-policy self-distillation method, allows for continuous policy refinement and can accelerate the discovery of solutions for challenging problems, while effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting of prior capabilities.
originalProblem: Current Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods suffer from a
credit-assignment bottleneck,
as sparse scalar rewards make it hard to identify specific errors in long generated sequences.
Many verifiable environments provide rich textual feedback (e.g., runtime errors, unit test failures) that is often underutilized by traditional RL methods.
Generating dense supervision typically requires external, stronger teacher models or expensive human labeling, which are often unavailable in online learning settings.
results: SDPO consistently outperformed GRPO baselines, achieving a 10x speedup in wall-clock training time and higher final accuracy (e.g., 76.8% vs. 54.3% on Chemistry) while producing over 3x shorter and more concise responses.
On competitive programming tasks, SDPO reached higher final accuracy (48.8% vs. 41.2%) and did so with 4x fewer generations than GRPO, also surpassing top commercial instruct models.

For hard, sparse-reward questions, SDPO significantly accelerated solution discovery, achieving a 53.2% discovery probability with approximately 3x fewer generations than best-of-k sampling and solving questions unsolveable by baselines.
solution: The paper formalizes Reinforcement Learning with Rich Feedback (RLRF) to explicitly leverage detailed, tokenized feedback from verifiable environments.
Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO) is introduced, an on-policy algorithm that uses the current LLM itself as a
self-teacher,
to provide dense, logit-level supervision when conditioned on rich feedback.
SDPO converts rich textual feedback into precise, token-level advantages for each possible next token, offering fine-grained credit assignment unlike sparse scalar rewards.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ETH Zurich, Max Planck Institute, MIT, and Stanford developed Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO), an on-policy algorithm that trains large language models by transforming rich environmental feedback into dense, token-level learning signals. This method yields a 10x speedup in training time, requires 4x fewer generations to reach baseline accuracy on competitive programming tasks, and enables solution discovery for hard problems with 3x fewer generations.

title: Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation

abstract: Teaching large language models (LLMs) to critique and refine their outputs is crucial for building systems that can iteratively improve, yet it is fundamentally limited by the ability to provide accurate judgments and actionable suggestions. In this work, we study LLM critics for code generation and propose $\texttt{CTRL}$, a framework for $\texttt{C}$ritic $\texttt{T}$raining via $\texttt{R}$einforcement $\texttt{L}$earning, which trains a critic model to generate feedback that maximizes correction performance for a fixed generator model without human supervision. Our results demonstrate that critics trained with $\texttt{CTRL}$ significantly enhance pass rates and mitigate compounding errors across both base and stronger generator models. Furthermore, we show that these critic models act as accurate generative reward models and enable test-time scaling through iterative critique-revision, achieving up to 106.1% relative improvements across challenging code generation benchmarks.

keyInsights: Decoupling the critic from the generator and training it with a specialized RL framework, guided by execution results, offers a robust and scalable solution to the feedback bottleneck.

Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) effectively addresses high variance and credit assignment issues in RL for complex feedback generation tasks, enabling stable learning in a vast critique space.

The research provides empirical evidence for 'weak-to-strong generalization' in LLM guidance, where a relatively weaker, specialized critic can effectively supervise and enhance the performance of a more capable, general-purpose generator.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with self-improvement due to a 'feedback bottleneck,' often generating non-actionable or inaccurate critiques that can degrade performance.
Existing feedback mechanisms like reward models offer simplified numerical signals, while automated verification tools provide low-level execution traces lacking high-level actionable guidance.
Directly training critique generation via reinforcement learning faces high variance in gradient estimates due to the vast space of possible critiques, leading to training instability.
results: CTRL achieved a 11.76% Pass@1 rate for single-turn critique-revision on CodeContests, showing a 49.2% relative improvement over zero-shot generation and significantly reducing the regression rate to 0.85%.

The framework demonstrated strong generalization to multi-turn settings, increasing Pass@1 to 15.15% (Critique×3) on CodeContests, and effectively guided a stronger model (GPT-4o) with up to 23.5% relative Pass@1 improvement.

CTRL mitigates compounding errors over iterations, maintaining a lower and more stable regression rate compared to baselines, and promotes more substantial, structural code revisions (mean code similarity 0.313 for CTRL vs. 0.482 for self-critique).
solution: CTRL employs a two-stage pipeline: first, supervised finetuning (SFT) synthesizes high-quality critiques by reasoning over objective execution feedback (from unit tests) for code generation.

Second, a reinforcement learning (RL) stage fine-tunes the critic's ability to maximize solution improvement using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to stabilize training by normalizing rewards within groups of critiques.
The critic model is decoupled from the generator, allowing specialized training to maximize the generator's correction performance without requiring human-annotated critique data.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from The University of Hong Kong and ByteDance introduced CTRL, a reinforcement learning framework for training a specialized LLM critic to provide effective and actionable feedback, particularly for code generation. This framework achieved up to 106.1% relative Pass@1 improvements on code benchmarks and demonstrated weak-to-strong generalization, where a weaker critic guides a stronger generator, without relying on human-annotated critiques.

title: Teaching Language Models to Critique via Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Code-generating Large Language Models (LLMs) have become essential tools in modern software development, enhancing productivity and accelerating development. This paper aims to investigate the fine-tuning of code-generating LLMs using Reinforcement Learning and Direct Preference Optimization, further improving their performance. To achieve this, we enhance the training data for the reward model with the help of symbolic execution techniques, ensuring more comprehensive and objective data. With symbolic execution, we create a custom dataset that better captures the nuances in code evaluation. Our reward models, fine-tuned on this dataset, demonstrate significant improvements over the baseline, CodeRL, in estimating the quality of generated code. Our code-generating LLMs, trained with the help of reward model feedback, achieve similar results compared to the CodeRL benchmark.

keyInsights: Symbolic execution substantially improves the quality and objectivity of training data for reward models, leading to critics that are significantly better at estimating code correctness.

A direct correlation between highly accurate reward models and superior code-generating actor performance is not automatically observed, suggesting other factors like actor training strategies play a critical role.
The approach effectively mitigates issues of human bias and incomplete test coverage in code evaluation, thereby refining the foundational feedback signal for LLM fine-tuning.
originalProblem: Human-created unit tests for fine-tuning code-generating LLMs often suffer from limited coverage and potential biases, leading to coarse feedback signals.
The quality of 'preference data' for Reinforcement Learning (RL) based LLM fine-tuning is often compromised by the incompleteness or redundancy of existing test cases.
Existing fine-tuning methods for code LLMs struggle to ensure comprehensive and objective evaluation during the training process.
results: Symbolic execution successfully increased the mean number of test cases per APPS task from 1 to 5 and the median from 5.16 to 7.22.
The `CodeRL-SE-critic` achieved an accuracy of 0.4250, a 37.19% increase over the baseline's 0.3098, with a significantly lower Mean Absolute Error of 0.6617.
The best actor model, `RL with CodeRL-SE-critic`, attained a pass@5 of 4.37, representing a marginal 0.14 absolute percentage point improvement over the `CodeRL` baseline (4.23).
solution: Employed symbolic execution, specifically the CrossHair tool, to automatically generate comprehensive and unbiased test cases for the APPS dataset, ensuring thorough Control Flow Graph path coverage.
Trained reward models (critics) using this symbolically enhanced dataset, comparing performance of a fine-tuned existing critic and a newly trained critic from scratch.
Fine-tuned code-generating LLMs (actors) using both Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) frameworks, leveraging feedback from the improved critics.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at TU Darmstadt developed a method to integrate symbolic execution into the fine-tuning process of code-generating Large Language Models. This approach significantly enhanced the accuracy of reward models, demonstrating a 37.19% improvement over the baseline, though its direct impact on the code-generating actor models' pass@5 scores was limited.

title: Integrating Symbolic Execution into the Fine-Tuning of Code-Generating LLMs

abstract: Large Language Models have achieved strong performance on reasoning tasks, solving competition-level coding and math problems. However, their scalability is limited by human-labeled datasets and the lack of large-scale, challenging coding problem training data. Existing competitive coding datasets contain only thousands to tens of thousands of problems. Previous synthetic data generation methods rely on either augmenting existing instruction datasets or selecting challenging problems from human-labeled data. In this paper, we propose QueST, a novel framework which combines difficulty-aware graph sampling and difficulty-aware rejection fine-tuning that directly optimizes specialized generators to create challenging coding problems. Our trained generators demonstrate superior capability compared to even GPT-4o at creating challenging problems that benefit downstream performance. We leverage QueST to generate large-scale synthetic coding problems, which we then use to distill from strong teacher models with long chain-of-thought or to conduct reinforcement learning for smaller models, proving effective in both scenarios. Our distillation experiments demonstrate significant performance gains. Specifically, after fine-tuning Qwen3-8B-base on 100K difficult problems generated by QueST, we surpass the performance of the original Qwen3-8B on LiveCodeBench. With an additional 112K examples (i.e., 28K human-written problems paired with multiple synthetic solutions), our 8B model matches the performance of the much larger DeepSeek-R1-671B. These findings indicate that generating complex problems via QueST offers an effective and scalable approach to advancing the frontiers of competitive coding and reasoning for large language models.

keyInsights: The self-consistency of a strong LLM's multiple solutions provides a reliable and verifiable proxy for estimating the difficulty of a generated problem.
Specialized LLM generators can be effectively trained using rejection fine-tuning and difficulty-aware sampling to create high-quality, challenging synthetic problems.
High-quality, difficult synthetic data can significantly boost the reasoning capabilities of smaller LLMs, enabling them to match or surpass much larger models on complex benchmarks.
originalProblem: The scarcity and high cost of human-generated, truly challenging training data bottleneck the advancement of large language models in complex reasoning tasks.
Existing competitive programming datasets are limited in size and difficulty, insufficient for training increasingly capable LLMs requiring diverse and sophisticated reasoning patterns.
Previous synthetic data generation methods mainly augment existing problems or curate solutions, failing to create novel and inherently difficult problems.
results: QueST successfully generated over 100K challenging coding problems, establishing the largest synthetic dataset for code reasoning.
An 8B model fine-tuned on QueST-generated data achieved a LiveCodeBench-V5 score of 65.2, matching the performance of the 671B DeepSeek-R1 model.

The proposed difficulty measure 
                                $\delta(q)$
                            was confirmed as effective, with models trained on problems having the highest 
                                $\delta$
                            achieving superior performance (39.28 LiveCodeBench-V5 score).
solution: QueST employs a preliminary problem generation scaffolding based on concept graphs derived from seed problems.

It introduces difficulty-aware rejection fine-tuning, which iteratively trains an LLM generator by retaining only the single most difficult problem from multiple candidates, as assessed by a verifiable difficulty metric.
Difficulty-aware graph sampling enhances the concept graph construction by incorporating human-annotated difficulty, biasing random walks towards challenging concept combinations.
paper_summary.summary: QueST, a framework from the University of Zurich and Microsoft Research, trains large language models to generate difficult competitive coding problems at an unprecedented scale. This enables an 8B model, fine-tuned on the generated synthetic data, to achieve code reasoning performance on par with a 671B model, establishing a new Pareto optimum.

title: QueST: Incentivizing LLMs to Generate Difficult Problems

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved impressive performance on code generation. However, for complex programming tasks, generating the correct solution in one go becomes challenging, thus some prior works have designed program repair approaches to improve code generation performance. In this work, we propose Self-Debugging, which teaches a large language model to debug its predicted program via few-shot demonstrations. In particular, we demonstrate that Self-Debugging can teach the large language model to perform rubber duck debugging; i.e., without any human feedback on the code correctness or error messages, the model is able to identify its mistakes by investigating the execution results and explaining the generated code in natural language. Self-Debugging achieves the state-of-the-art performance on several code generation benchmarks, including the Spider dataset for text-to-SQL generation, TransCoder for C++-to-Python translation, and MBPP for text-to-Python generation. On the Spider benchmark where there are no unit tests to verify the correctness of predictions, Self-Debugging with code explanation consistently improves the baseline by 2-3%, and improves the prediction accuracy on problems of the hardest level by 9%. On TransCoder and MBPP where unit tests are available, Self-Debugging improves the baseline accuracy by up to 12%. Meanwhile, by leveraging feedback messages and reusing failed predictions, Self-Debugging notably improves sample efficiency, and can match or outperform baseline models that generate more than 10x candidate programs.

keyInsights: Large language models can perform iterative self-correction on code, effectively acting as debuggers for their own outputs.
The 'rubber duck debugging' principle, where an LLM explains its own code, helps it identify and fix bugs even without explicit external error messages.
Combining internal self-explanation with objective execution feedback (e.g., unit test results) significantly enhances the model's debugging performance across various coding tasks.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) often struggle to generate perfectly correct and functional code in a single attempt, particularly for complex programming tasks.
Current methods to improve code correctness, such as generating many candidate programs and reranking them, are computationally expensive and sample-inefficient.
LLMs lack a built-in, iterative debugging capability similar to human programmers, who refine their code by identifying and fixing errors.
results: The SELF-DEBUGGING framework achieved state-of-the-art performance on Spider (text-to-SQL), TransCoder (C++-to-Python translation), and MBPP (text-to-Python generation) benchmarks, for example, reaching 84.1% accuracy on Spider with Codex.
Significant improvements in sample efficiency were observed; for instance, self-debugging applied to a single initial prediction on Spider matched the accuracy of a baseline sampling 16 programs.

Code explanation feedback alone improved accuracy by 2-3% on Spider across different initial sample counts and by 9% on its hardest problems, while unit test feedback provided substantial gains, such as a 12% boost on TransCoder.
solution: A SELF-DEBUGGING framework is proposed that iteratively refines an LLM's code predictions through a cycle of generation, explanation, and feedback, without requiring additional model training.
The approach leverages few-shot prompting to guide the LLM to explain its own generated code (mimicking 'rubber duck debugging') and to interpret various forms of feedback.

Different types of feedback are incorporated, including binary correctness, specific unit test results (runtime errors, output mismatches), natural language code explanations, and model-generated execution traces.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Google DeepMind and UC Berkeley developed SELF-DEBUGGING, an iterative prompting framework that enables large language models to autonomously identify and correct errors in their generated code. This approach, which incorporates 'rubber duck debugging' through self-explanation and external execution feedback, achieves state-of-the-art performance and significantly improves sample efficiency across various coding tasks like text-to-SQL and code translation.

title: Teaching Large Language Models to Self-Debug

abstract: Common self-improvement approaches for large language models (LLMs), such as STaR, iteratively fine-tune LLMs on self-generated solutions to improve their problem-solving ability. However, these approaches discard the large amounts of incorrect solutions generated during this process, potentially neglecting valuable information in such solutions. To address this shortcoming, we propose V-STaR that utilizes both the correct and incorrect solutions generated during the self-improvement process to train a verifier using DPO that judges correctness of model-generated solutions. This verifier is used at inference time to select one solution among many candidate solutions. Running V-STaR for multiple iterations results in progressively better reasoners and verifiers, delivering a 4% to 17% test accuracy improvement over existing self-improvement and verification approaches on common code generation and math reasoning benchmarks with LLaMA2 models.

keyInsights: Utilizing both correct and incorrect self-generated solutions provides a richer learning signal, making the self-improvement process more data-efficient for LLMs.
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a highly effective and sample-efficient method for training verifiers, allowing them to specialize in evaluation with rapid improvement.

An integrated, co-evolving generator and verifier system leads to mutually reinforcing improvements, where advancements in one component drive gains in the other, enhancing overall reasoning capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing LLM self-improvement methods are data-inefficient, discarding valuable incorrect solutions during fine-tuning.
Traditional verifier training often operates independently of the iterative improvement process of the generator, limiting their co-evolution.
Improving LLM problem-solving accuracy on complex multi-step reasoning tasks like math and code generation remains a challenge.
results: Achieved 6% to 17% absolute improvement in test accuracy on GSM8K (math) and 4% to 12% on MBPP (code) over existing self-improvement and verification baselines.
A 7B V-STaR model matched or surpassed the performance of LLaMA2 70B on GSM8K and approached CodeLLaMA 34B's performance on HumanEval.
DPO-trained verifiers consistently outperformed Outcome-supervised Reward Model (ORM) verifiers, especially with a higher number of candidate solutions.
solution: V-STaR implements an iterative loop that progressively refines both an LLM generator (reasoner) and its accompanying verifier.
It uniquely leverages *all* generated solutions (both correct and incorrect) to train the verifier using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).
The generator is fine-tuned on self-generated correct solutions, while the DPO verifier learns to distinguish correct from incorrect solutions collected from the iteratively improving generator.
paper_summary.summary: V-STaR introduces an iterative framework that co-trains an LLM generator and verifier by utilizing both correct and incorrect self-generated solutions. This approach significantly enhances performance on complex reasoning tasks, enabling smaller models to achieve capabilities comparable to or exceeding much larger base models.

title: V-STaR: Training Verifiers for Self-Taught Reasoners

abstract: Large Language Models excel at code generation yet struggle with complex programming tasks that demand sophisticated reasoning. To bridge this gap, traditional process supervision relies on learned reward models requiring costly training data and suffering from reward misalignment, while outcome supervision fails for complex tasks needing coordinated intermediate steps. We introduce Outcome Refining Process Supervision, which unifies process and outcome supervision by leveraging executable verification: a tree-structured search framework generates strategic alternatives, profiles execution metrics, and scores candidates via self-critique mechanisms that integrate runtime feedback with reasoning. Experiments across 5 models and 3 benchmarks show consistent gains, with 26.9% higher correctness and 42.2% improved code efficiency. The results demonstrate that ORPS enables LLMs to overcome local optima in code generation, suggesting a promising direction for combining verifiable outcomes with structured reasoning to tackle complex challenges. We open-source at: this https URL

keyInsights: Large language models' (LLMs) intrinsic reasoning and self-critique capabilities, when grounded by verifiable execution feedback, can generate high-quality process rewards without the need for separately trained Process Reward Models (PRMs).
Providing sufficient reasoning space for algorithmic exploration is often more critical for complex code generation than merely scaling model parameters.

Unifying objective execution metrics with subjective self-critique through a combined process and outcome reward score (qt = αρt + βωt) enables LLMs to explore and select superior algorithmic strategies.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) struggle with complex, multi-step algorithmic reasoning in code generation tasks like dynamic programming.
Existing outcome supervision provides sparse pass/fail feedback, neglecting crucial intermediate reasoning steps.

Traditional process supervision, relying on trained Process Reward Models (PRMs), faces high data annotation costs, reliability issues like hallucination and reward hacking, and poor generalization for code-specific tasks.
results: The Outcome-Refining Process Supervision (ORPS) framework achieved an average Pass@1 improvement of 26.9% and an average running time reduction of 42.2% across five LLMs and three programming benchmarks.
Smaller models, when enhanced with ORPS, frequently outperformed larger models using conventional methods, demonstrating that strategic reasoning can be more critical than pure model scale.
Ablation studies confirmed that the inference-only ORPS approach significantly outperformed all trained Process Reward Models, supporting the claim that dedicated PRMs are not necessary.
solution: The Outcome-Refining Process Supervision (ORPS) framework is introduced as a novel, inference-only method that unifies process and outcome supervision for code generation.
It employs a tree-structured search, allowing for parallel exploration of diverse algorithmic strategies, where each node represents an iterative refinement of reasoning and code.

The LLM acts as both a 'programmer' to generate candidate solutions and a 'critic' to evaluate execution feedback and self-critique, generating both objective outcome rewards (from execution) and subjective process rewards (from self-critique).
paper_summary.summary: Peking University and William & Mary researchers developed Outcome-Refining Process Supervision (ORPS), a framework for large language models (LLMs) that unifies process and outcome rewards for complex code generation tasks. ORPS leverages LLMs' intrinsic reasoning and self-critique capabilities with verifiable execution feedback, achieving an average Pass@1 improvement of 26.9% and an average running time reduction of 42.2% across diverse LLMs and programming benchmarks.

title: Reasoning Through Execution: Unifying Process and Outcome Rewards for Code Generation

abstract: Code generation has attracted increasing attention with the rise of Large Language Models (LLMs). Many studies have developed powerful code LLMs by synthesizing code-related instruction data and applying supervised fine-tuning. However, these methods are limited by teacher model distillation and ignore the potential of iterative refinement by self-generated code. In this paper, we propose Adaptive Critique Refinement (ACR), which enables the model to refine itself by self-generated code and external critique, rather than directly imitating the code responses of the teacher model. Concretely, ACR includes a composite scoring system with LLM-as-a-Judge to evaluate the quality of code responses and a selective critique strategy with LLM-as-a-Critic to critique self-generated low-quality code responses. We develop the RefineCoder series by iteratively applying ACR, achieving continuous performance improvement on multiple code generation benchmarks. Compared to the baselines of the same size, our proposed RefineCoder series can achieve comparable or even superior performance using less data.

keyInsights: LLMs can achieve continuous, intrinsic improvement in their one-shot code generation capabilities by iteratively learning from their own generated code combined with adaptive, targeted critiques.

A robust composite scoring system, integrating multi-faceted LLM judgment with code execution results, is critical for accurately evaluating code quality and providing effective feedback for self-correction.

The study highlights the impact of data leakage in commonly used benchmarks, suggesting that some state-of-the-art performances may be inflated, and emphasizes the importance of using clean, contamination-free datasets for accurate evaluation.
originalProblem: Existing Distillation-based Fine-Tuning (DFT) approaches inherently limit student model performance to the teacher's capabilities, neglecting the potential for models to improve through introspection.
Inference-time self-refinement methods, while improving single generations, do not fundamentally alter or enhance the LLM's core ability to produce high-quality code in a single pass.
Many iterative training approaches rely on costly golden labels or extensive preference data, which can be difficult to acquire at scale and limit data efficiency.
results: RefineCoder-DS-6.7B and RefineCoder-QW-7B demonstrated continuous Pass@1 improvements across three iterations on challenging benchmarks (LiveCodeBench, BigCodeBench-hard), achieving average improvements of 2.7 and 3.1 points respectively, and surpassing existing baselines.

The models showed strong out-of-distribution generalization, with RefineCoder-QW-7B (Iter3) improving multilingual code generation on the MultiPL-E benchmark by 2.1 points despite being fine-tuned on a Python-only dataset.

RefineCoder models effectively incorporate external feedback (execution and human-like suggestions), with RefineCoder-QW-7B (Iter1 and Iter3) even surpassing GPT-4o on three benchmarks when provided with human-like improvement suggestions.
solution: The Adaptive Critique Refinement (ACR) paradigm iteratively refines an existing dataset with self-generated code and external critique to fine-tune Large Language Models (LLMs).

ACR involves a four-stage loop: sampling multiple diverse code responses, ranking them using a composite scoring system (LLM-as-a-Judge + code executor), selectively critiquing (LLM-as-a-Critic generates two-turn feedback), and training the model from scratch on the refined dataset.

The method adaptively constructs new training data by either reinforcing successful self-generated code or generating critiques for inferior self-generated code based on a superior reference, enabling the model to learn from its errors.
paper_summary.summary: RefineCoder introduces Adaptive Critique Refinement (ACR), an iterative fine-tuning paradigm that enhances Large Language Models' intrinsic one-shot code generation ability by systematically evaluating self-generated code with an LLM-as-a-Judge and LLM-as-a-Critic, then incorporating targeted feedback into subsequent training. The method demonstrates continuous performance improvements on coding benchmarks and robust generalization, often outperforming larger models and methods affected by data leakage.

title: RefineCoder: Iterative Improving of Large Language Models via Adaptive Critique Refinement for Code Generation

abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have improved their performance on coding benchmarks. However, improvement is plateauing due to the exhaustion of readily available high-quality data. Prior work has shown the potential of synthetic self-instruct data, but naively training on a model's own outputs can cause error accumulation, especially in coding tasks, where generalization may collapse due to overly simple or erroneous training data, highlighting the need for rigorous quality checks on synthetic data. In this work, we explore an effective approach whereby the model itself verifies the correctness of its own data. We thus propose Sol-Ver, a self-play solver-verifier framework that jointly improves a single model's code and test generation capacity. By iteratively refining code (LLM-as-a-solver) and tests (LLM-as-a-verifier) together, we boost both capabilities without relying on human annotations or larger teacher models. Experiments with the Llama 3.1 8B model demonstrate substantial performance enhancements, achieving average relative improvements of 19.63% in code generation and 17.49% in test generation on MBPP and LiveCodeBench.
title: Learning to Solve and Verify: A Self-Play Framework for Code and Test Generation

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown significant advancements in code generation, but still face challenges on tasks beyond their basic capabilities. Recently, the notion of self-debugging has been proposed to boost the performance of code generation by leveraging execution feedback from tests. Despite its promise, the availability of high-quality tests in real-world scenarios is limited. In this context, self-debugging with self-generated tests is a promising solution but lacks a full exploration of its limitations and practical potential. Therefore, we investigate its efficacy on diverse programming problems. To deepen our understanding, we propose two distinct paradigms for the process: post-execution and in-execution self-debugging. Within the scope of self-contained Python programming tasks, we find that post-execution self-debugging struggles on basic problems but shows potential for improvement on competitive ones, due to the bias introduced by self-generated tests. On the other hand, in-execution self-debugging enables LLMs to mitigate the bias by solely leveraging intermediate states during execution, thereby enhancing code generation.

organization_info.name: Key Laboratory of High Confidence Software Technology (PKU)

keyInsights: The primary bottleneck for effective post-execution self-debugging with self-generated tests is the low reliability and bias of the LLM-generated test outputs, particularly the expected results.
Self-generated tests frequently produce false negatives, where correct programs are incorrectly flagged as faulty, leading LLMs to make detrimental 'fixes'.
In-execution self-debugging offers a more robust feedback mechanism by focusing on internal program behavior, thereby mitigating the negative impact of unreliable test outputs.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) often fail to produce fully correct code for complex programming tasks, necessitating post-generation refinement.
Traditional self-debugging approaches for LLMs primarily rely on scarce and costly human-written 'oracle tests' for feedback.
The efficacy and practical limitations of LLMs debugging their own code solely with self-generated tests remain underexplored and poorly understood.
results: Post-execution self-debugging with self-generated tests showed limited to negative pass rate improvements on basic problems, with LLM-generated test suites achieving only 44-59% overall accuracy for expected outputs.
The efficacy of post-execution self-debugging varies significantly, often degrading performance on easier problems and showing mixed results on competitive programming tasks.
In-execution self-debugging consistently yielded improvements in pass rates across both basic (e.g., GPT-4o on MBPP+ from 76.5% to 79.1%) and competitive programming problems for most evaluated LLMs.
solution: The research proposes and formally defines two self-debugging paradigms based on self-generated tests: post-execution and in-execution.
Post-execution self-debugging utilizes feedback available after program completion, such as output comparison and error messages.
In-execution self-debugging leverages intermediate runtime states, specifically variable values within basic code blocks, observed during program execution.
paper_summary.summary: A study from Peking University and Meituan investigates how large language models (LLMs) can debug their own code using self-generated tests, defining two paradigms: post-execution and in-execution. The findings indicate that while post-execution methods are hampered by unreliable self-generated test outputs, in-execution self-debugging, leveraging intermediate runtime states, shows consistent pass rate improvements, such as GPT-4o increasing from 76.5% to 79.1% on MBPP+.

title: Revisit Self-Debugging with Self-Generated Tests for Code Generation

abstract:
In the domain of code generation, self-debugging is crucial. It allows LLMs
to refine their generated code based on execution feedback. This is
particularly important because generating correct solutions in one attempt
proves challenging for complex tasks. Prior works on self-debugging mostly
focus on prompting methods by providing LLMs with few-shot examples, which work
poorly on small open-sourced LLMs. In this work, we propose LeDex, a training
framework that significantly improves the self-debugging capability of LLMs.
Intuitively, we observe that a chain of explanations on the wrong code followed
by code refinement helps LLMs better analyze the wrong code and do refinement.
We thus propose an automated pipeline to collect a high-quality dataset for
code explanation and refinement by generating a number of explanations and
refinement trajectories from the LLM itself or a larger teacher model and
filtering via execution verification. We perform supervised fine-tuning (SFT)
and further reinforcement learning (RL) on both success and failure
trajectories with a novel reward design considering code explanation and
refinement quality. SFT improves the pass@1 by up to 15.92% and pass@10 by
9.30% over four benchmarks. RL training brings additional up to 3.54%
improvement on pass@1 and 2.55% improvement on pass@10. The trained LLMs show
iterative refinement ability and can keep refining code continuously. Lastly,
our human evaluation shows that the LLMs trained with our framework generate
more useful code explanations and help developers better understand bugs in
source code.
title: LeDex: Training LLMs to Better Self-Debug and Explain Code

abstract: Modern instruction-tuned large language models (LLMs) have made remarkable progress in code generation. However, these LLMs fine-tuned with standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) sometimes generate plausible-looking but functionally incorrect code variants. This issue likely stems from the limitation of standard SFT, which treats all tokens equally during optimization and fails to emphasize the error-sensitive segments-specific code differences between correct implementations and similar incorrect variants. To address this problem, we propose Fault-Guided Fine-Tuning (FGIT), a novel fine-tuning technique that enhances LLMs' code generation by (1) extracting multi-granularity (line/token-level) differences between correct and incorrect yet similar implementations to identify error-sensitive segments, and (2) dynamically prioritizing those segments during training via dynamic loss weighting. Through extensive experiments on seven LLMs across three widely-used benchmarks, our method achieves an average relative improvement of 6.9% on pass@1 with some enhanced 6.7B LLMs outperforming closed-source models, e.g., GPT-3.5-Turbo. Furthermore, our fine-tuning technique demonstrates strong generalization with performance improvements ranging from 3.8% to 19.1% across diverse instruction-tuned LLMs, and our ablation studies confirm the contributions of different granularities of differences and hyperparameters.
title: FGIT: Fault-Guided Fine-Tuning for Code Generation

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have advanced code generation from single-function tasks to competitive-programming problems, but existing multi-agent solutions either rely on costly large-scale (>30B) models or collapse when downsized to small open-source models. We present MapCoder-Lite, a framework for distilling the complex reasoning of large, multi-agent coding systems into a single 7B model. Our contribution is a novel, three-pillar methodology that synergistically generates, refines, and encodes multi-agent knowledge: (i) pass-based trajectory distillation from strong LLMs fixes format fragility in retrieval and reduces failures in debugging, (ii) supervisor-guided correction with global feedback strengthens planning and coding agents, and (iii) agent-wise LoRA fine-tuning delivers memory-efficient specialisation. Comprehensive evaluation on xCodeEval, APPS, and CodeContests shows that MapCoder-Lite more than doubles xCodeEval accuracy (from 13.2% to 28.3%), eliminates all format failures, while reducing GPU memory and token-generation time by 4x compared to a 32B model. It also achieves over 10% gains on simpler coding benchmarks, demonstrating broad improvements beyond competitive programming. These results demonstrate that careful agent-wise fine-tuning unleashes high-quality multi-agent coding on a small language model. Our code is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Agent-wise fine-tuning, specifically tailored for each role in a multi-agent pipeline, enables small LLMs to overcome structured output non-adherence and improve task-specific reasoning capabilities.

Distilling knowledge from powerful LLMs, combined with filtering based on the end-to-end success of generated code, is crucial for creating effective and contextually aware training data for SLMs in complex tasks.

A single small LLM can emulate the complex, multi-stage reasoning of systems composed of much larger LLMs through targeted fine-tuning, significantly reducing computational resource demands without compromising performance.
originalProblem: Multi-agent code generation systems for complex tasks like competitive programming primarily rely on massive LLMs (>30B parameters), leading to high computational costs, long runtimes, and limited accessibility.

Small language models (SLMs, <10B parameters) deployed in multi-agent frameworks struggle with adhering to structured output formats (e.g., XML) and lack sufficient capacity for complex role-specific reasoning.

Fine-tuning SLMs for multi-agent tasks faces challenges due to a scarcity of high-quality, role-specific training data, limited global awareness across agents, and the inefficiency of traditional full-parameter tuning.
results: MapCoder-Lite achieved over 2x accuracy increase on xCodeEval (from 13.21% to 28.30%) and improvements on APPS (6.00% to 8.00%) and CodeContests (6.06% to 13.33%) compared to the untuned framework.
The system reduced GPU memory usage by 4x and time per output token by 4x compared to a 32B parameter model, while completely eliminating XML-schema format failures across benchmarks.

It demonstrated strong generalization, boosting HumanEval accuracy from 70.73% to 82.93% and MBPP from 67.51% to 84.63%, and proved compatible with diverse LLM families including Llama3.1-8B and CodeLlama-7B.
solution: A three-pillar methodology distills multi-agent knowledge into a single 7B parameter LLM via pass-based trajectory distillation, supervisor-guided cross-agent refinement, and memory-efficient LoRA specialization.
High-quality, execution-verified intermediate outputs are collected from powerful LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-32B, DeepSeek-V3) using pass-based filtering to ensure end-to-end problem-solving success.
A larger supervisor LLM (DeepSeek-V3) provides targeted, role-specific feedback to the 7B model for refinement during data generation, addressing capacity gaps and enhancing global awareness.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Hanyang University and Samsung SDS developed MapCoder-Lite, a framework that distills multi-agent code generation capabilities into a single 7-billion-parameter language model using role-aligned fine-tuning. This approach achieves performance comparable to 14B or 32B parameter models while reducing GPU memory usage by 4x and time per output token by 4x.

title: MapCoder-Lite: Distilling Multi-Agent Coding into a Single Small LLM

abstract: Can language models improve their reasoning performance without external rewards, using only their own sampled responses for training? We show that they can. We propose Self-evolving Post-Training (SePT), a simple post-training method that alternates between self-generation and training on self-generated responses. It repeatedly samples questions, uses the model itself to generate low-temperature responses, and then finetunes the model on the self-generated data. In this self-training loop, we use an online data refresh mechanism, where each new batch is generated by the most recently updated model. Across six math reasoning benchmarks, SePT improves a strong no-training baseline, defined as the untuned base model evaluated at its best swept decoding temperature, on several tested models. In some settings, SePT can even approach the performance of Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). Additional ablations demonstrate the importance of online data refresh and temperature decoupling. Overall, our results identify a practical regime in which reasoning can be improved using self-generated supervision alone. Our code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

title: A Model Can Help Itself: Reward-Free Self-Training for LLM Reasoning

abstract: Despite the effectiveness of large language models (LLMs) for code generation, they often output incorrect code. One reason is that model output probabilities are often not well-correlated with correctness, and reflect only the final output of the generation process. Inspired by findings that LLMs internally encode concepts like truthfulness, this paper explores if LLMs similarly represent code correctness. Specifically, we identify a correctness representation inside LLMs by contrasting the hidden states between pairs of correct and incorrect code for the same programming tasks. By experimenting on four LLMs, we show that exploiting this extracted correctness representation outperforms standard log-likelihood ranking, as well as verbalized model confidence. Furthermore, we explore how this internal correctness signal can be used to select higher-quality code samples, without requiring test execution. Ultimately, this work demonstrates how leveraging internal representations can enhance code generation systems and make LLMs more reliable, thus improving confidence in automatically generated code.
title: On LLMs' Internal Representation of Code Correctness

abstract:
Self-improving large language models (LLMs) -- i.e., to improve the
performance of an LLM by fine-tuning it with synthetic data generated by itself
-- is a promising way to advance the capabilities of LLMs while avoiding
extensive supervision. Existing approaches to self-improvement often rely on
external supervision signals in the form of seed data and/or assistance from
third-party models. This paper presents Crescent -- a simple yet effective
framework for generating high-quality synthetic question-answer data in a fully
autonomous manner. Crescent first elicits the LLM to generate raw questions via
a bait prompt, then diversifies these questions leveraging a rejection
sampling-based self-deduplication, and finally feeds the questions to the LLM
and collects the corresponding answers by means of majority voting. We show
that Crescent sheds light on the potential of true self-improvement with zero
external supervision signals for math reasoning; in particular,
Crescent-generated question-answer pairs suffice to (i) improve the reasoning
capabilities of an LLM while preserving its general performance (especially in
the 0-shot setting); and (ii) distil LLM knowledge to weaker models more
effectively than existing methods based on seed-dataset augmentation.
title: The Self-Improvement Paradox: Can Language Models Bootstrap Reasoning  Capabilities without External Scaffolding?

abstract:
Prompting techniques such as chain-of-thought have established themselves as
a popular vehicle for improving the outputs of large language models (LLMs).
For code generation, however, their exact mechanics and efficacy are
under-explored. We thus investigate the effects of a wide range of prompting
strategies with a focus on automatic re-prompting over multiple turns and
computational requirements. After systematically decomposing reasoning,
instruction, and execution feedback prompts, we conduct an extensive grid
search on the competitive programming benchmarks CodeContests and TACO for
multiple LLM families and sizes (Llama 3.0 and 3.1, 8B, 70B, 405B, and GPT-4o).
Our study reveals strategies that consistently improve performance across all
models with small and large sampling budgets. We then show how finetuning with
such an optimal configuration allows models to internalize the induced
reasoning process and obtain improvements in performance and scalability for
multi-turn code generation.

keyInsights: CoT prompting significantly enhances performance for more capable models and harder problems, but combining too many reasoning prompts or using them inefficiently can degrade results.
Multi-turn interactions alone yield modest gains; integrating CoT is essential for substantial performance improvements, with adaptive strategies like CoT-retry being most effective.

The granularity of execution feedback influences LLM behavior: fine-grained feedback leads to exploitative local fixes, while simpler feedback encourages exploratory, diverse solutions, often better for complex algorithmic challenges.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) for code generation often struggle with complex algorithmic reasoning tasks found in competitive programming.
Existing state-of-the-art approaches either rely on massive single-turn sampling or intricate multi-step prompt chains, lacking a systematic understanding of multi-turn efficacy.
There is a need to understand the precise benefits and computational costs of various prompting strategies in multi-turn code generation, especially when accounting for LLM interaction costs.
results: CoT-retry emerged as the most effective multi-turn CoT strategy for Llama 3.1 models, outperforming both single-turn and multi-turn-only baselines.

Rejection Sampling Finetuning (RFT) on multi-turn CoT data led to a Llama 3.1 70B model achieving superior performance to the best inference-time prompting configurations, even without explicit CoT prompts at inference.
The RFT model exhibited increased sampling diversity, enhanced self-repair capabilities over longer trajectories, and generalized its improved reasoning to various code generation benchmarks.
solution: A unified framework was developed to systematically deconstruct and combine different prompting components: reasoning prompts, instruction prompts, and execution feedback prompts within single and multi-turn settings.
The study introduced a 'CoT-retry' strategy, applying Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompts adaptively only when an initial solution fails in a multi-turn interaction.

Rejection Sampling Finetuning (RFT) was employed to enable models to internalize the beneficial reasoning processes by finetuning on successful multi-turn CoT trajectories with explicit CoT prompts removed.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Meta AI and Paris Dauphine University demonstrated that large language models can internalize complex reasoning for code generation through Rejection Sampling Finetuning on successful multi-turn Chain-of-Thought trajectories. This approach achieved performance superior to inference-time prompting, leading to intrinsically more capable code LLMs that require less explicit prompting during deployment.

title: What Makes Large Language Models Reason in (Multi-Turn) Code Generation?

abstract: Distilling the thinking traces of a Large Language Model (LLM) with reasoning capabilities into a smaller model has been proven effective. Yet, there is a scarcity of work done on how model performances scale with the quantity of distillation data. In this work, we study the scaling trend of distilling competitive coding skills on two small non-reasoning LLMs. We validate the hypothesis that there is a $\textit{valley of code reasoning}$: downstream performance on competitive coding first drops as data quantity increases, then it steadily increases in a sharper-than-log-linear fashion. Having identified the trend, we further fine-tune the models at two different distillation stages on the same data to ground conclusions on their respective learning phases. We learn that across stages in the low and medium-low data regimes, small models benefit significantly from easier coding questions than from harder ones. We also find that, surprisingly, the correctness of outputs in training data makes no difference to distillation outcomes. Our work represents a step forward in understanding the training dynamics of code reasoning distillation outside intuition
title: The Valley of Code Reasoning: Scaling Knowledge Distillation of Large Language Models

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown outstanding breakthroughs in code
generation. Recent work improves code LLMs by training on synthetic data
generated by some powerful LLMs, which can be challenging to scale due to the
dependence on a teacher model and high generation costs. In this paper, we
focus on synthesizing code data at scale and propose a \textbf{Case2Code} task
by exploiting the expressiveness and correctness of programs.
\textbf{Case2Code} is an inductive inference task that aims to infer underlying
code implementations by observing input-output examples or program behaviors,
By incorporating LLMs to generate program inputs, and executing the program
with these inputs to obtain the program outputs, we can synthesize diverse and
high-quality \textbf{Case2Code} data at scale for training and evaluating code
LLMs. Experimental results show that case-to-code induction is challenging for
current representative LLMs if they are untrained. Models trained with
\textbf{Case2Code} improve performance not only on distribution case-to-code
induction but also on various coding-generation tasks, demonstrating the great
potential of large-scale synthetic data and inductive learning.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs) trained primarily on deductive code generation struggle significantly with inductive reasoning tasks like inferring code from input-output examples.
Training LLMs on Case2Code data not only enhances their inductive reasoning but also leads to improvements in general code generation capabilities, suggesting positive transfer learning.
The framework's approach, using weaker LLMs to generate inputs and a code interpreter for outputs, enables highly scalable and cost-effective synthetic data generation for code.
originalProblem: Existing methods for generating synthetic code data are costly and unscalable, heavily relying on powerful, often proprietary, 'teacher' LLMs for code generation.
Large Language Models exhibit poor performance on inductive inference tasks (e.g., Programming by Example) due to the scarcity of such examples in their training datasets.
There is a need for diverse and challenging synthetic data to complement current code training data and push LLMs beyond instruction-following towards more complex reasoning.
results: Untrained LLMs show a substantial performance gap, with LLaMA3-70B achieving 34.0% on Case2Code compared to 77.4% on HumanEval, and GPT-4 scoring 43.6% on Case2Code vs. 90.2% on HumanEval.
Fine-tuning with Case2Code data improved inductive reasoning accuracy by up to 18.9% (InternLM2-7B and LLaMA3-8B), enabling smaller models to achieve scores comparable to larger models.
Training on Case2Code data generalized to improve HumanEval scores, with InternLM2-7B increasing from 49.4% to 64.6% and LLaMA3-8B from 49.8% to 64.8% after mixed instruction-tuning.
solution: Case2Code introduces a framework to synthesize inductive reasoning data by collecting human-written Python functions from The Stack and filtering them for executability.

It uses an LLM to generate diverse input examples for these functions and a fast code interpreter to execute them, obtaining corresponding output examples, thereby decoupling data generation from code generation.

The generated input-output pairs are post-processed and embedded into natural language prompts using diverse templates to create Case2Code-style training data, which explicitly targets inductive reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University and Shanghai AI Laboratory developed Case2Code, a scalable framework for generating synthetic input-output examples to train large language models (LLMs) for inductive code reasoning. This approach, which is more cost-effective than prior methods, improved LLMs' ability to infer code from examples and also enhanced their general code generation performance on benchmarks like HumanEval.

title: Case2Code: Scalable Synthetic Data for Code Generation

abstract: Instruction tuning is a supervised fine-tuning approach that significantly improves the ability of large language models (LLMs) to follow human instructions. We propose SelfCodeAlign, the first fully transparent and permissive pipeline for self-aligning code LLMs without extensive human annotations or distillation. SelfCodeAlign employs the same base model for inference throughout the data generation process. It first extracts diverse coding concepts from high-quality seed snippets to generate new tasks. It then samples multiple responses per task, pairs each with test cases, and validates them in a sandbox environment. Finally, passing examples are selected for instruction tuning. In our primary experiments, we use SelfCodeAlign with CodeQwen1.5-7B to generate a dataset of 74k instruction-response pairs. Finetuning on this dataset leads to a model that achieves a 67.1 pass@1 on HumanEval+, surpassing CodeLlama-70B-Instruct despite being ten times smaller. Across all benchmarks, this finetuned model consistently outperforms the original version trained with OctoPack, the previous state-of-the-art method for instruction tuning without human annotations or distillation. Additionally, we show that SelfCodeAlign is effective across LLMs of various sizes, from 3B to 33B, and that the base models can benefit more from alignment with their own data distribution. We further validate each component's effectiveness in our pipeline, showing that SelfCodeAlign outperforms both direct distillation from GPT-4o and leading GPT-3.5-based distillation methods, such as OSS-Instruct and Evol-Instruct. SelfCodeAlign has also led to the creation of StarCoder2-Instruct, the first fully transparent, permissively licensed, and self-aligned code LLM that achieves state-of-the-art coding performance.

keyInsights: A base LLM can learn more effectively from its own self-generated data than from data generated by a stronger 'teacher' model, especially when the performance gap between them is not substantial, highlighting the importance of data distribution alignment.

Execution-based filtering, where only instruction-response pairs whose generated code successfully passes its self-generated test cases are retained, is critical for achieving high-quality self-alignment.

Rigorous selection of high-quality seed functions and a structured approach to instruction generation (via 'concepts extraction') significantly improve the diversity and utility of the self-generated training data.
originalProblem: Instruction tuning of code LLMs typically relies on costly human annotation or knowledge distillation from proprietary, non-permissively licensed LLMs like GPT-4.
Many high-performing instruction-tuned code LLMs lack transparency regarding their tuning strategies or use proprietary data, hindering reproducibility and community growth.
Prior self-alignment attempts for code generation, such as direct application of SELF-INSTRUCT, often resulted in performance degradation, indicating a need for code-specific adaptations.
results: SelfCodeAlign-CQ-7B achieves 67.1 pass@1 on HumanEval+, outperforming CodeLlama-70B-Instruct (65.2) and surpassing state-of-the-art GPT-3.5-Turbo based distillation methods, while closely rivaling GPT-4o distillation (65.9).

The approach consistently improves performance across various LLM sizes (3B to 33B parameters) and on diverse benchmarks including function-level generation (MBPP+, LiveCodeBench), class-level generation (ClassEval), and code editing (CanItEdit).

This work led to the creation of StarCoder2-Instruct, which is highlighted as the first fully transparent and permissively licensed self-aligned code LLM achieving state-of-the-art coding performance (e.g., 72.6 HumanEval score, surpassing CodeLlama-70B-Instruct's 72.0).
solution: A multi-stage, iterative self-alignment pipeline is developed, using the same base LLM for all steps to ensure transparency and independence from external proprietary models.
The process begins with collecting and rigorously filtering high-quality, diverse Python functions as 'seed snippets' from open-source code corpora.

The base LLM generates diverse coding tasks (instructions) from these seeds, along with multiple response candidates and self-generated test cases, which are then validated through sandbox execution to filter for correctness.
paper_summary.summary: SelfCodeAlign introduces a fully transparent and permissibly licensed pipeline for self-aligning code Large Language Models (LLMs), enabling them to generate high-quality code without extensive human annotation or reliance on proprietary models. The SelfCodeAlign-CQ-7B model, fine-tuned with this approach, achieves 67.1 pass@1 on HumanEval+, outperforming CodeLlama-70B-Instruct (65.2) and rivaling GPT-4o distillation (65.9).

title: SelfCodeAlign: Self-Alignment for Code Generation

abstract:
The potential for pre-trained large language models (LLMs) to use natural
language feedback at inference time has been an exciting recent development. We
build upon this observation by formalizing an algorithm for learning from
natural language feedback at training time instead, which we call Imitation
learning from Language Feedback (ILF). ILF requires only a small amount of
human-written feedback during training and does not require the same feedback
at test time, making it both user-friendly and sample-efficient. We further
show that ILF can be seen as a form of minimizing the KL divergence to the
ground truth distribution and demonstrate a proof-of-concept on a neural
program synthesis task. We use ILF to improve a Codegen-Mono 6.1B model's
pass@1 rate by 38% relative (and 10% absolute) on the Mostly Basic Python
Problems (MBPP) benchmark, outperforming both fine-tuning on MBPP and
fine-tuning on repaired programs written by humans. Overall, our results
suggest that learning from human-written natural language feedback is both more
effective and sample-efficient than training exclusively on demonstrations for
improving an LLM's performance on code generation tasks.

organization_info.name: HiTZ Center, University of the Basque Country (UPV/EHU)

title: Improving Code Generation by Training with Natural Language Feedback

abstract: Existing code large language models (LLMs) often rely on large-scale instruction data distilled from proprietary LLMs for fine-tuning, which typically incurs high costs. In this paper, we explore the potential of small-scale open-source LLMs (e.g., 7B) as synthesizers for high-quality code instruction data construction. We first observe that the data synthesis capability of small-scale LLMs can be enhanced by training on a few superior data synthesis samples from proprietary LLMs. Building on this, we propose a novel iterative self-distillation approach to bootstrap small-scale LLMs, transforming them into powerful synthesizers that reduce reliance on proprietary LLMs and minimize costs. Concretely, in each iteration, to obtain diverse and high-quality self-distilled data, we design multi-checkpoint sampling and multi-aspect scoring strategies for initial data selection. Furthermore, to identify the most influential samples, we introduce a gradient-based influence estimation method for final data filtering. Based on the code instruction datasets from the small-scale synthesizers, we develop SCoder, a family of code generation models fine-tuned from DeepSeek-Coder. SCoder models achieve state-of-the-art code generation capabilities, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method.
title: SCoder: Iterative Self-Distillation for Bootstrapping Small-Scale Data Synthesizers to Empower Code LLMs

abstract: Code LLMs still struggle with code execution reasoning, especially in smaller models. Existing methods rely on supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with teacher-generated explanations, primarily in two forms: (1) input-output (I/O) prediction chains and (2) natural-language descriptions of execution traces. However, intermediate execution steps cannot be explicitly verified during SFT, so the training objective can reduce to merely matching teacher explanations. Moreover, training data is typically collected without explicit control over task difficulty. We introduce ExecVerify, which goes beyond text imitation by incorporating verifiable white-box rewards derived from execution traces, including next-statement prediction and variable value/type prediction. Our work first builds a dataset with multiple difficulty levels via constraint-based program synthesis. Then, we apply reinforcement learning (RL) to reward correct answers about both intermediate execution steps and final outputs, aligning the training objective with semantic correctness at each execution step. Finally, we adopt a two-stage training pipeline that first enhances execution reasoning and then transfers to code generation. Experiments demonstrate that a 7B model trained with ExecVerify achieves performance comparable to 32B models on code reasoning benchmarks and improves pass@1 by up to 5.9\% on code generation tasks over strong post-training baselines.

keyInsights: Integrating verifiable white-box rewards directly from execution traces for intermediate steps (e.g., next statement, variable state) effectively guides LLMs to internalize program execution logic, moving beyond mere textual imitation.
A structured, curriculum-style dataset, synthesized with constraints and filtered by difficulty, significantly enhances training efficiency and the quality of learned execution reasoning for LLMs.

The fine-grained execution knowledge acquired through white-box RL successfully transfers to improve downstream code generation tasks, demonstrating that deeper reasoning leads to more functionally correct code.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs), especially smaller ones, struggle with concrete program execution reasoning due to training data primarily consisting of static text rather than dynamic execution semantics.

Existing Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) methods for execution understanding do not explicitly verify intermediate execution steps, leading models to learn superficial textual patterns instead of robust semantic logic.
Training datasets for code LLMs often lack structured difficulty control, resulting in a mix of trivial or unsolvable examples that hinder effective, curriculum-style learning.
results: A 7B model trained with ExecVerify achieved an average score of 80.8 on execution reasoning benchmarks, a 20.0-point improvement over the base Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct model (60.8).

The two-stage RL pipeline yielded improvements in code generation, with the best variant achieving an average pass@1 score of 57.1, an increase of up to 5.9 points over a pure code generation RL baseline.
ExecVerify demonstrated cross-language generalization on CRUXEval-X and consistent improvements across different LLM architectures and model sizes (3B, 7B, 8B), showing broad applicability.
solution: A constraint-based data synthesis method generates a high-quality, curriculum-style dataset of executable Python snippets with controlled difficulty and structural diversity through iterative prompting and filtering.

A two-stage post-training pipeline first employs SFT warm-up, followed by white-box Reinforcement Learning (RL) using explicit, verifiable rewards for fine-grained execution steps (control-flow and data-flow questions).
The second stage applies RL for code generation, using unit-test-based rewards to align the enhanced execution reasoning with functional correctness for generated code.
paper_summary.summary: ExecVerify introduces a white-box reinforcement learning framework that uses verifiable, stepwise rewards from execution traces to enhance LLM understanding of program execution. A 7B model trained with ExecVerify improved execution reasoning by 20.0 points over its base model and boosted code generation pass@1 by up to 5.9 points.

title: ExecVerify: White-Box RL with Verifiable Stepwise Rewards for Code Execution Reasoning

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in code generation tasks. However, their effectiveness heavily relies on supervised training with extensive labeled (e.g., question-answering pairs) or unlabeled datasets (e.g., code snippets), which are often expensive and difficult to obtain at scale. To address this limitation, this paper introduces a method IPC, an unsupervised framework that leverages Internal Probing of LLMs for Code generation without any external corpus, even unlabeled code snippets. We introduce the problem space probing, test understanding probing, solution space probing, and knowledge consolidation and reinforcement to probe the internal knowledge and confidence patterns existing in LLMs. Further, IPC identifies reliable code candidates through self-consistency mechanisms and representation-based quality estimation to train UCoder (coder with unsupervised learning). We validate the proposed approach across multiple code benchmarks, demonstrating that unsupervised methods can achieve competitive performance compared to supervised approaches while significantly reducing the dependency on labeled data and computational resources. Analytic experiments reveal that internal model states contain rich signals about code quality and correctness, and that properly harnessing these signals enables effective unsupervised learning for code generation tasks, opening new directions for training code LLMs in resource-constrained scenarios.

keyInsights: Pre-trained Large Language Models possess sufficient latent programming knowledge to autonomously generate high-quality problems, test cases, and correct solutions for self-improvement.
Correct code implementations for a given problem will consistently produce identical outputs for all inputs, allowing unsupervised correctness verification through execution-driven consensus.
Unsupervised self-training can be a compute-efficient alternative to simply scaling up model size, as smaller models show disproportionately larger performance gains through this method.
originalProblem: The prohibitive cost and logistical difficulties of acquiring large-scale, high-quality supervised instruction data for training and fine-tuning code generation LLMs.
Existing unsupervised code post-training methods still rely on vast external unlabeled code corpora, presenting a dependency on external data.
The fundamental challenge of enabling LLMs to autonomously improve their code generation capabilities without any external corpus.
results: UCoder models achieved performance comparable to, and often exceeding, state-of-the-art supervised instruction-tuned models across diverse benchmarks, with UCoder-7B reaching 85.2% on MBPP (outperforming Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct's 83.5%).
The iterative self-bootstrapping process consistently improved performance by +6.1 to +13.2 points for 7B models, +4.3 to +10.6 for 14B models, and +3.0 to +4.9 for 32B models on various benchmarks.

Self-generated problems exhibited high lexical diversity (Shannon entropy μ = 3.64 bits) and semantic coverage (229 domain-specific terms across 7 categories), demonstrating a broad and natural distribution of programming concepts.
solution: A six-stage self-bootstrapping framework, called Internal Probing of LLMs for Code generation (IPC), which forms a positive feedback loop.
LLMs autonomously generate diverse programming problems, comprehensive test cases, and a dense sampling of candidate solutions for each problem.

An execution-driven consensus clustering mechanism verifies solution correctness by grouping candidate solutions with identical execution signatures against generated tests, selecting the largest cluster as the reliable 'gold standard'.
paper_summary.summary: UCoder introduces a fully unsupervised framework that enables Large Language Models to improve code generation capabilities by internally generating programming problems, test cases, and high-quality solutions, completely independent of external data. It achieves performance competitive with supervised models, with UCoder-7B reaching 85.2% on MBPP and UCoder-32B demonstrating 89.0% on HumanEval without human annotations.

title: UCoder: Unsupervised Code Generation by Internal Probing of Large Language Models

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently advanced many applications on software engineering tasks, particularly the potential for code generation. Among contemporary challenges, code generated by LLMs often suffers from inaccuracies and hallucinations, requiring external inputs to correct. One recent strategy to fix these issues is to refine the code generated from LLMs using the input from the model itself (self-augmented). In this work, we proposed a novel method, namely CoT-SelfEvolve. CoT-SelfEvolve iteratively and automatically refines code through a self-correcting process, guided by a chain of thought constructed from real-world programming problem feedback. Focusing on data science code, including Python libraries such as NumPy and Pandas, our evaluations on the DS-1000 dataset demonstrate that CoT-SelfEvolve significantly outperforms existing models in solving complex problems. The framework shows substantial improvements in both initial code generation and subsequent iterations, with the model's accuracy increasing significantly with each additional iteration. This highlights the effectiveness of using chain-of-thought prompting to address complexities revealed by program executor traceback error messages. We also discuss how CoT-SelfEvolve can be integrated into continuous software engineering environments, providing a practical solution for improving LLM-based code generation.

organization_info.name: Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology

keyInsights: Combining structured Chain-of-Thought reasoning with relevant external knowledge from developer forums significantly improves an LLM's ability to self-correct complex data science code, mimicking human debugging processes.

Leveraging a more powerful LLM (e.g., GPT-4) specifically for generating Auto-CoT prompts, even when a less powerful LLM performs the code generation, leads to a notable performance gain (e.g., 11.26% relative improvement).
The most substantial accuracy improvements from iterative self-correction occur in the initial attempts, indicating the high value of early feedback and refinement steps.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently generate inaccurate or "hallucinated" code, especially for complex data science tasks, requiring extensive manual debugging.
Existing LLM self-correction methods often struggle with subtle logical errors and intricate code dependencies, failing to effectively leverage real-world debugging knowledge.
Current automated program repair (APR) techniques typically operate under a "near-correct assumption" and face difficulties generating diverse patches for complex bugs.
results: CoT-SelfEvolve demonstrated an 83.8% pass@5 for GPT-4 on the DS-1000 dataset, with substantial improvements over SelfEvolve in PyTorch (89.71% vs. 70.59%) and Sklearn (97.39% vs. 70.43%).
The Auto-CoT prompt generators contributed a 16.39% relative performance improvement, with the initial generation prompt having a slightly greater impact than the refinement prompt.
Increasing iterative attempts from 1 to 5 boosted GPT-4's pass@n from 14.0% to 83.2%, though total prompt token consumption rose from 2.54 million to 9.29 million tokens for five attempts.
solution: The CoT-SelfEvolve framework integrates Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting with an external knowledge base derived from StackOverflow into an iterative self-correction loop.

It employs two Auto-CoT prompt generators: one for initial code generation using external knowledge, and another for refining code based on syntax checks and execution feedback (tracebacks, output discrepancies).
A structured external knowledge base from StackOverflow posts and comments is built, embedded, and stored in a vector database to provide relevant context for LLM prompts.
paper_summary.summary: CoT-SelfEvolve introduces a framework that enhances Large Language Model performance in generating and correcting data science code by integrating Chain-of-Thought prompting and external knowledge from StackOverflow. The framework achieved up to 83.2% pass@5 for GPT-4 on the DS-1000 benchmark, outperforming previous self-correction methods on various Python libraries.

title: An Empirical Study on Self-correcting Large Language Models for Data Science Code Generation

abstract:
Large language models demonstrate exceptional performance in simple code
generation tasks but still face challenges in tackling complex problems. These
challenges may stem from insufficient reasoning and problem decomposition
capabilities. To address this issue, we propose a reasoning-augmented data
generation process, SRA-MCTS, which guides the model to autonomously generate
high-quality intermediate reasoning paths. This creates a positive feedback
loop, enabling continuous improvement. Our method operates entirely through the
model itself without requiring additional supervision. By synthesizing natural
language reasoning paths and translating them into executable code, the
approach ensures analytical accuracy and enhances the success rate in solving
complex tasks. Experimental results show that, even without additional
supervisory signals, our method achieves performance improvements across
different model scales, demonstrating the significant potential of
